humanities_role_explanation: Designed for evaluating figurative language understanding and idiomaticity representation, which are core topics in linguistics and semantic analysis.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Integrates natural language sentences containing potentially idiomatic nominal compounds with generated static images (Subtask A) or sequential image panels representing visual narratives (Subtask B). It also offers a text-only modality using descriptive image captions.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Tasks require identifying whether a nominal compound is used in a literal or figurative context, ranking generated illustrations based on semantic alignment, and predicting sequential visual outcomes of idiomatic scenarios.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Specifically targets idiomaticity and figurative language understanding rather than broad general-purpose capabilities.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates several generative models including GPT-4, Qwen, LLaVA, DeepSeek, and various CLIP/VLM configurations.

llm_is_primary_system.note: LLMs and vision-language models are the primary systems evaluated by participating teams.

quantitative_metrics.note: Top 1 Accuracy, Discounted Cumulative Gain (DCG), Image Completion Accuracy, and Sentence Type Accuracy.

published_after_march_2023.note: Published in 2025 as part of SemEval-2025.

english_language.note: The paper and dataset are written in English (with Portuguese also supported in the dataset).

summary: This paper introduces AdMIRe (SemEval-2025 Task 1), a benchmark designed to evaluate how well vision-language models represent idiomatic expressions in English and Brazilian Portuguese. The benchmark features two tasks: ranking images based on their alignment with idiomatic or literal context sentences, and predicting the next image in a narrative sequence. The top-performing submissions used mixture-of-experts settings with large language and vision models to achieve human-level performance.
key_findings: The top-performing systems achieved up to 93% Top-1 accuracy on the English Subtask A test set and 100% on the Portuguese test set, matching or slightly exceeding human-expert baselines (human average of 71% accuracy, pool-of-experts at 83%). Achieving this performance required complex mixture-of-experts pipelines, data augmentation, and targeted prompt engineering to mitigate generative models' inherent biases towards literal interpretations.
